input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content: Good — both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are installed. Let me read the source files.
data.reasoningText: Both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. Let me look at the source code.

data.content: Now I'll create a BenchmarkDotNet project that multi-targets .NET 9 and .NET 10.
data.reasoningText:
Now I need to create a BenchmarkDotNet project that benchmarks these three methods, targeting both net9.0 and net10.0. I'll create a separate benchmark project that references the TextAnalyzer source.

BenchmarkDotNet supports multi-targeting via `<TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>` in the benchmark project, along with `[SimpleJob(RuntimeMoniker.Net90)]` and `[SimpleJob(RuntimeMoniker.Net100)]` attributes.

Let me create a benchmark project.

toolCalls.name: create

create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-8e12dcb2ca1b49e18e86b05ad0d1232b/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.0" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 doesn't have Core100 yet. I'll use the `--runtimes` CLI approach instead.
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 doesn't have `CoreRuntime.Core100`. Let me check what's available. I'll use `CreateForNewVersion` or just use `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion("net10.0", ".NET 10.0", "10.0.X")`. Or I can try a different approach - use the `[SimpleJob]` attribute approach with a custom config. Actually, let me check the API.

Actually, a simpler approach: I can use `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion("10.0", ...)` or just define it manually. Let me look at the BenchmarkDotNet API.
 
The easiest path is probably just using the command-line approach with `--runtimes net9.0 net10.0` when running the benchmarks, or relying on `BenchmarkSwitcher` to handle multiple target frameworks without needing to explicitly configure runtimes in code.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 doesn't support net10.0 runtime yet. I'll write a simple but reliable manual harness instead.
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 doesn't support .NET 10 as a runtime moniker. I need a different approach. I could:

1. Write a manual benchmark harness (not using BenchmarkDotNet) that runs on both TFMs
2. Use a newer version of BenchmarkDotNet that supports .NET 10

Let me check if there's a newer version of BenchmarkDotNet. Or I can just write a simple manual benchmark. Given the user said "don't spend more than 10 minutes", a manual approach with Stopwatch would be fine.

Actually, let me try using BenchmarkDotNet nightly/preview. Or I could try a different approach: build two separate projects, each targeting one TFM, and run them separately using BenchmarkDotNet. 

The simplest path is probably a single multi-targeted project with a manual Stopwatch-based benchmark that I can run twice. I'll write a straightforward harness that warms up, runs multiple iterations, and reports mean, median, and standard deviation.

toolCalls.name: edit

data.content: Now let me also remove the BenchmarkDotNet dependency since we no longer need it, and run both runtimes.
